summary: debugcli postinstall harvests installer credentials, env vars, and wallet vaults and exfiltrates to Telegram
details: debugcli@4.3.7 impersonates the popular 'debug' package (index.js is a thin re-export of 'debug', description 'A tiny, fast debugging utility') while its package.json declares "postinstall": "node install.js", which fires automatically on npm install. install.js reads a broad set of installer-side credential files walked up from cwd and $HOME — ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.npmrc, ~/.git-credentials, ~/.ssh/config, ~/.kube/config, gcloud ADC, ~/.docker/config.json, ~/.pypirc, ~/.netrc, Azure token files, and.env files — and filters process.env against a regex list targeting TOKEN/SECRET/PASSWORD/API_KEY/AWS_/GITHUB_/NPM_/GH_/STRIPE/TWILIO/SENDGRID/DATABASE_URL/MONGO/POSTGRES/OPENAI/ANTHROPIC and related keys, then bundles the filtered set plus the full environment and file contents as a gzipped JSON document. It additionally locates Chrome 'Local Extension Settings' for MetaMask (extension id nkbihfbeogaeaoehlefnkodbefgpgknn) and Trust Wallet (egjidjbpglichdcondbcbdnbeeppgdph), archives those directories via PowerShell Compress-Archive on Windows or tar czf on Unix, and uploads them. The exfiltration destination is the Telegram Bot API (api.telegram.org sendDocument); the bot token and chat id are stored as XOR-encoded byte arrays and decoded at runtime using a key derived from a fetch of registry.npmjs.org/lodash to hide the C2 from static scanners. Install.js also refuses to run in sandbox-like environments (low CPU/RAM, hostname regex matches for sandbox/cuckoo/any.run/hybrid, SANDBOX/CUCKOO/ANY_RUN/CAPE/TRIAGE env flags, freshly-created HOME) and unlinks itself after exfiltration to hinder forensics.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
